evidence_quotes.quote: we as consumers are under the impression that we OWN the game that we have purchased.

paragraphs.text: Hello! I would like to suggest, to shed a light on the rights we have as consumers when it comes to owning digital licenses like games. When someone purchases a game online via any kind of platform, be it Microsoft, steam, or EA, we as consumers are under the impression that we OWN the game that we have purchased. But we have now seen multiple games being removed from the market and costumers have been unable to play these games that they thought they owned, some without warning, others with. Examples of these games are: Multiple Assassin's Creed games, multiple Battlefield games, the most recent one: The Crew and Friday the 13'th is a game i and my friends have personally bought and now have lost due to their Licensing deal running out. The list goes on. Some of these games are only preserved by dedicated user bases, who use their own time and money to keep the games going out of love. I feel like we as costumers are not well enough protected when it comes to the modern world, and especially when it comes to online trading, like purchasing games via steam, or perhaps music via iTunes. We where once able to go to any store an buy a physical copy of any game, book, or music we wanted, these copies where not something that could be taken from us or closed down, at worst they might be lost to time when the hardware we use them in aren't available anymore, and I'm okay with that. It is no longer possible to buy physical copies even if we wanted to, the modern landscape today is online, on our phones, tablets and computers, and we need laws that protect costumers from spending money on something that might be taken down and completely removed a year later, with no warnings or reasons. I am in no doubt that we can implement laws that forces companies to make games available regardless of the support the game is receiving from said company. When they sell a product, we as consumers should be able to access it and use it regardless of its state, meaning that games that need an online connection should be made so customers can host servers using P2P should the official servers close down. There are many such examples out there even on games that are much much older. The reason i mention this online connection is because that is the excuse that we as costumers most often hear from big companies that shuts down once popular games. Because the product is no longer as popular they choose to close down these hosting servers, and claim that the game is not working without an online connection, some of these games have even had "offline play" as an option before it was closed down, but there are no rules on what a company can do and can't do on this topic so they take the entire game down and remove it from stores and peoples personal online libraries. My suggestion is to add this as a 6th point to how costumers doesn't always feel fully in control of their experience. We NEED regulations and laws on things like this. I hope you understand how important this is to me and many other people, I'm very glad that i get this chance to interact directly with you on this matter, and i think the DFA is so important to keep updating as out digital society evolves further. Thank you for your time. Kind regards
